extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r4, which requires (bradycardia AND heart_failure AND hypertension). rec_drug_b is set true only by r3, which requires bradycardia. These two rules write to DIFFERENT outputs (rec_drug_a vs rec_drug_b), so the per-output priority conflict resolution never suppresses one in favor of the other—priorities only arbitrate among rules targeting the same output. With bradycardia=true, heart_failure=true, hypertension=true: r3 fires (rec_drug_b:=true) and r4 fires (rec_drug_a:=true) simultaneously. low_ejection_fraction and immunosuppressed are irrelevant to this property (they only affect rec_drug_c); set to false. Result: rec_drug_a=true AND rec_drug_b=true, violating the mutual-exclusion property. The plain-language intent (never recommend both drug a and drug b) is breached because the rule set guarantees that any patient triggering drug a (bradycardia + heart failure + hypertension) necessarily also triggers drug b (bradycardia is a strict subset condition).
